summary: American hyperrealist sculptor known for lifelike depictions of ordinary Americans. Used resin, fiberglass, and lifecasting techniques to create uncannily realistic figures. Major works include Tourists, Young Shopper, Supermarket Shopper, and Queenie II. Part of the photorealism/hyperrealism movement alongside Chuck Close and Richard Estes.

works.name: Tourists (1970)
works.description: Depicts a camera-toting man in a Hawaiian shirt standing next to his wife. One of Hanson's most iconic works. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Depicts a camera-toting man in a Hawaiian shirt standing next to his wife
examples: An artist from this movement depicted a camera-toting man in a Hawaiian shirt standing next to his wife in the 1970 sculpture Tourists.
This other photorealist used materials like resin and fibreglass to create works like Young Shopper, Tourists

works.name: Supermarket Shopper
works.description: Sculpture of an overweight woman with hair curlers pushing a shopping cart, created using lifecasting. Also known as Young Shopper in some references. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Sculpture of an overweight woman with hair curlers and a shopping cart, created using lifecasting
examples: Duane Hanson used this method to make Supermarket Shopper, a sculpture of an overweight woman with hair curlers and a shopping cart.
A "lady" with prominent varicose veins and curlers in her hair holds her hands against one of these objects in a photorealist sculpture by Duane Hanson.

clues.clue: Created using lifecasting technique (casting from a live model)
examples: Duane Hanson used this method to make Supermarket Shopper, a sculpture of an overweight woman with hair curlers and a shopping cart. Auguste Rodin was accused of using this method to create The Age of Bronze.

works.name: Queenie II
works.description: Sculpture of a black janitorial worker. One of Hanson's works depicting working-class Americans.

clues.clue: Sculpture of a black janitorial worker
examples: This other photorealist used materials like resin and fibreglass to create works like Young Shopper, Tourists and one of a black janitorial worker, Queenie II.

clues.clue: George Segal created white plaster figures at a diner; Hanson created fully realistic sculptures of people at a diner
examples: George Segal and Duane Hanson respectively created white plaster figures and fully realistic sculptures of people at one of these locations.

comprehensive_summary: Duane Hanson was an American hyperrealist sculptor best known for creating uncannily lifelike sculptures of everyday Americans. He used materials like resin and fiberglass, along with lifecasting (casting directly from live models), to produce figures that blur the line between art and reality. His most frequently referenced work is Tourists (1970), depicting a camera-wielding man in a Hawaiian shirt standing beside his wife — a commentary on American middle-class culture. Supermarket Shopper (also referenced as Young Shopper) shows an overweight woman with hair curlers and varicose veins pushing a shopping cart, and is often cited in connection with the lifecasting technique. Queenie II portrays a black janitorial worker. Hanson is consistently grouped within the hyperrealism/photorealism movement, alongside painters Chuck Close and Richard Estes. He is sometimes contrasted with George Segal, who created white plaster figures, whereas Hanson's sculptures are painted and clothed to appear fully realistic.
